title: FG auctions forfeited drug kingpins’ properties for N6.1bn
description: The Federal Government has auctioned four properties forfeited to it by convicted drug traffickers for a total of N6.15 billion. The assets, including a six-floor hotel in Lagos, which were seized from drug kingpins by the National Drug Law Enforcement Agency (NDLEA) and forfeited after court processes, were sold through a competitive bidding process conducted […]
content:
The Federal Government has auctioned four properties forfeited to it by convicted drug traffickers for a total of N6.15 billion.
The assets, including a six-floor hotel in Lagos, which were seized from drug kingpins by the National Drug Law Enforcement Agency (NDLEA) and forfeited after court processes, were sold through a competitive bidding process conducted at the agency’s headquarters in Abuja on Monday.
NDLEA’s Director of Media and Advocacy, Femi Babafemi, who disclosed this in a statement, said the sale followed a competitive bidding process involving eight forfeited assets across different parts of the country.
While four properties attracted successful bids above the reserved prices, bids for the remaining four failed to meet the benchmark and were not approved.
Speaking at the bid-opening ceremony held at the NDLEA’s headquarters in Abuja, the Head of Asset Recovery and Management Unit of the Federal Ministry of Justice, Tamarantare Francis Ali-Bozi, announced the successful bidders.
Daily Trust reports that Tope Ojo and Tunde Olonishakin Estate Firm emerged the highest bidder for a six-floor hotel located in Victoria Island, Lagos, with an offer of N5.9 billion.

Other successful bidders included FSS Limited, which secured a property in Lekki Phase 1, Lagos, for N219.5 million; A-BNB Global Innovations Limited, which won a block of flats in Ejigbo, Lagos, for N104 million; and Fazeen Global Link Limited, which acquired a property in Akure, Ondo State, for N29.36 million.

The Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of the NDLEA, retired Brig.-Gen. Mohamed Buba Marwa, said the auction underscored the agency’s commitment to ensuring that proceeds of crime do not remain in the hands of criminals.

Represented by the agency’s Secretary, Shadrach Haruna, Marwa said public auctions of recovered assets serve a broader national purpose beyond generating revenue for the government.

“Public auctions such as this reinforce public confidence in the rule of law, demonstrate accountability in the management of recovered assets and send a clear message that criminal proceeds will not be allowed to remain in the hands of those who seek to profit from the misery and destruction caused by illicit drugs,” he said.

Marwa said the agency would continue to pursue drug traffickers, dismantle criminal networks and recover proceeds derived from illicit drug activities.

He also assured stakeholders that the auction process was conducted transparently and in line with the provisions of the Proceeds of Crime (Recovery and Management) Act, 2022, the Public Procurement Act, 2007, and other relevant regulations.

According to him, all the assets were professionally valued by the Federal Ministry of Housing and Urban Development, while due diligence and security screening were conducted on all auctioneers pre-qualified by the Bureau of Public Procurement (BPP).

Representatives of the Independent Corrupt Practices and Other Related Offences Commission (ICPC), civil society organisations, auctioneers and bidders attended the exercise.
summary: The Federal Government has auctioned four properties forfeited from convicted drug traffickers for a total of N6.15 billion, with a six-floor hotel in Lagos selling for N5.9 billion, indicating a significant transaction in the Lagos real estate market that may reflect growing demand for high-value properties. This could mean increased competition for luxury developments in areas like Victoria Island and Lekki. The sale of these properties for substantial amounts suggests a strong market for high-end real estate.
trending_topics: luxury real estate, property auctions, anti-money laundering

mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: The successful auction of high-end properties may indicate a strong and competitive market for luxury developments, which could impact Mixta's positioning and pricing strategy for projects like Lakowe Crossings and Lagos New Town.
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Mixta could explore opportunities in the luxury segment, particularly in areas like Victoria Island and Lekki, where demand seems high.

title: Nigerians shifting from home ownership to rentals – Chairman, Exclusive Estates
description:
Explore why Nigerians are shifting from home ownership to rentals. Discover the challenges in Nigeria’s real estate sector, including high costs and naira

Read More: https://punchng.com/nigerians-shifting-from-home-ownership-to-rentals-chairman-exclusive-est…
content: Over the next five years, Nigeria’s property market will be shaped by a mixture of significant risks and extraordinary opportunities. On the risk side, persistent inflation, exchange rate volatility, high borrowing costs, and rising construction expenses could continue to put pressure on both developers and homebuyers. These factors increase project costs, reduce affordability, and can slow the pace of real estate development. Regulatory uncertainties, delays in obtaining approvals, and infrastructure deficits also remain challenges that may discourage investment if not adequately addressed. Additionally, weakening consumer purchasing power could affect demand in certain segments of the property market, particularly among middle-income earners. Despite these challenges, the opportunities far outweigh the risks. Nigeria’s rapidly growing population, increasing urbanisation, and substantial housing deficit create a strong and sustained demand for residential accommodation. Emerging cities and development corridors are opening up new investment opportunities, while technological innovations in property marketing, management, and financing are making the sector more efficient and accessible. The growing interest in affordable housing, mixed-use developments, short-let accommodation, and institutional real estate investment also presents significant prospects for growth. If government and private-sector stakeholders work together to improve infrastructure, expand access to housing finance, and streamline regulatory processes, Nigeria’s real estate sector could become one of the country’s most powerful drivers of economic growth, job creation, and wealth generation. For investors with a long-term perspective, the future of the Nigerian property market remains highly promising. When evaluating a real estate investment or development opportunity, I pay close attention to a combination of macroeconomic and market-specific indicators. One of the first factors I consider is inflation because it directly affects construction costs, property values, and rental income. Interest rates are equally important, as they influence the cost of financing for both developers and buyers. Exchange rate stability is another critical consideration, particularly in Nigeria, where many building materials and construction inputs are either imported or affected by foreign exchange fluctuations. I also assess broader economic indicators such as gross domestic product growth, employment levels, and household income trends because they provide insight into the purchasing power of potential buyers and tenants. Beyond the macroeconomic environment, I examine demographic and location-specific factors. Population growth, urbanisation rates, and migration patterns often indicate future demand for housing and commercial space. Infrastructure developments such as new roads, rail systems, airports, and utility projects can significantly enhance property values and attract investment to a
summary: The article reports on the shifting trends in Nigeria's property market, with a focus on the challenges and opportunities over the next five years, and it means that the Lagos real estate market may experience increased demand for rentals and affordable housing, with potential growth opportunities in emerging cities and development corridors. The article highlights a substantial housing deficit and a rapidly growing population, with increasing urbanisation. This could lead to a strong and sustained demand for residential accommodation, potentially benefiting Mixta's projects such as Lakowe Crossings and Lagos New Town.
trending_topics: affordable housing, urbanisation, rental market, emerging cities

mixta_relevance.indirect_impact: Increased demand for affordable housing and rentals could benefit Mixta's position in the Lagos market, particularly for projects like Lakowe Crossings and Lagos New Town
mixta_relevance.strategic_opportunity: Potential for Mixta to explore emerging cities and development corridors for new investment opportunities
mixta_relevance.risk_flag: Regulatory uncertainties and infrastructure deficits could pose a risk to project execution and timelines
